context: the just shall live by faith
point_summary: True success, by God's measure, is defined by faith—taking God at His word—a principle the sermon traces from Habakkuk 2:4 ("the just shall live by faith") through Joshua and Hebrews 11:6.

reference_text: The just shall live by faith
context: But we've been called to live by faith. The just shall live by faith.
point_summary: Faith's call in Habakkuk 2:4 — "the just shall live by faith" — is invoked as the governing principle that believers are to simply take God at His word rather than demanding to understand how outcomes will unfold.

reference_text: the just shall live by his faith
context: the just shall live by faith
point_summary: Habakkuk 2:4's declaration that "the just shall live by faith" frames the entire call to combat unbelief by immersing in Scripture, since faith grows through hearing God's Word.

reference_text: the just shall live by faith
context: And so if we understand the just shall live by faith, and we understand where faith comes from from God's word, we need to have our faith increased.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

reference_text: the just shall live by faith
context: Therefore, the just shall live by faith.
point_summary: This present earthly life is the only opportunity believers have to live by faith, since sight replaces faith in heaven.

reference_text: The just shall live by faith
context: The just shall live by faith. We go by faith believing that God will use his Word.
point_summary: Living by faith—not human strategy or scheming—is the basis on which believers obey God's command to go forward, trusting him alone to accomplish what only he can do.

context: although the fig tree shall not blossom... neither shall fruit be in the vines...
point_summary: Habakkuk's declaration — "yet I will rejoice in the Lord" — is held up as a model of choosing rejoicing even when every visible circumstance, from failed harvests to empty stalls, paints a dim picture.

context: the labor of the olive shall fail, the field shall yield no meat, the flock shall be cut off from the fold, and there shall be no herd in the stalls.
point_summary: Even when every material circumstance is stripped away — no harvest, no flock, no provision — Habakkuk's deliberate, purposed declaration "yet I will rejoice in the Lord" models a joy rooted in God's character, not in conditions.

context: yet I will rejoice in the Lord, I will joy in the God of my salvation.
point_summary: Even amid total agricultural and economic devastation catalogued in verse 17, Habakkuk's "yet I will rejoice in the Lord" is presented as a purposed, intentional determination to rejoice independent of circumstances.

context: The Lord God is my strength and He will make my feet like Heinz feet and He will make me to walk upon mine high places to the chief singer on my stringed instruments.
point_summary: Even amid total agricultural and economic devastation catalogued in verse 17, Habakkuk's declaration in verse 19 models the intentional, purposed resolve to rejoice in God independent of circumstances.

context: yet I will rejoice in the Lord
point_summary: Habakkuk's "yet I will rejoice" in verse 18 is held up as a model of deliberate, resolved rejoicing chosen in spite of adverse circumstances, not conditioned on them.

reference_text: the just shall live by faith
context: And we understand the quote from Habakkuk that is referenced not only here in the book of Hebrews, but also Paul uses it in Romans and again in Galatians, that the just shall live by faith.
point_summary: Habakkuk 2:4's declaration that "the just shall live by faith" is cited as the foundational challenge underlying Hebrews 11, which then supplies Old Testament exemplars who embodied that very principle across every era of human history.

context: yet a little while. and he that shall come will come and will not tarry.
point_summary: The Old Testament principle from Habakkuk 2:3–4 — "the just shall live by faith" — is cited as the foundation underlying Hebrews 10:38–39, and later taken up distinctly by Paul in Romans and Galatians.

context: Now the just shall live by faith, but if any man draw back, my soul shall have no pleasure in him.
point_summary: The Old Testament principle "the just shall live by faith" from Habakkuk 2:4 is shown to carry distinct emphases across Romans (justification), Galatians (faith itself), and Hebrews (persevering endurance).

context: The Bible tells us that the just shall live by faith
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

reference_text: The just shall live by faith
context: The just, the Bible tells us, are to live by faith.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.
